title: Information in a Measurement Result & Instrument Characteristics
summary: Lessons 1 & 3. A measurement is never exact: its information is **distorted by systematic factors and confused by random ones**, so a result is incomplete unless paired with its **uncertainty interval**. You will learn the VIM vocabulary the exam uses — *measurand*, systematic vs random errors, *trueness*, *precision* (repeatability vs reproducibility), *accuracy* — and the metrological characteristics of instruments: *resolution*, *sensitivity*, *stability*, *linearity*, *hysteresis*, *zero drift*, plus calibration, traceability and metrological confirmation.
objectives: Explain why every measurement result must be given as a value plus an uncertainty interval, and use the overlap (compatible) / separation (different) rule to compare two results.

Define the measurand and distinguish systematic factors/errors (constant or predictable) from random factors/errors (unpredictable), and list the methods to reduce systematic effects: correction, compensation, elimination.
Distinguish trueness (closeness to a reference, inversely related to bias), precision (closeness among replicates), and accuracy (closeness to the true value, the total picture).
Distinguish repeatability conditions (same procedure, operator, instrument, short time) from reproducibility conditions (different operators, instruments, locations).
Define and compute the metrological characteristics: resolution, sensitivity, stability, range/linearity, hysteresis and drift.
Explain calibration, metrological traceability through the unbroken calibration chain, and the role of the GUM and VIM.

blocks.text: 1. The information contained in a measurement

We measure **in order to know**. But experimental data are **not an absolute truth**: repeating the same measurement several times you obtain different results, so you can never get an **exact result**.

Every measurement result is influenced by a multiplicity of factors:
- **Systematic factors** — big and regular enough that the relevant cause can be individuated;
- **Random factors** — small, irregular, not describable in a deterministic way, so they must be treated statistically.

The information contained in a measure can only be extracted remembering that its numeric data is always **distorted by systematic factors** and **confused by the random ones**.

blocks.title: A result without its uncertainty is incomplete
blocks.text: Two gauge blocks of equal nominal length read **identically** on a digital micrometer, yet a higher-resolution instrument (e.g. an LVDT) shows they differ. The paradox comes from the false idea that a block has one defined **true value**. Operatively we move to a **statistical vision** and give a **range of values** around the result in which the value of the measurand reasonably lies — the **uncertainty interval**. The information given by a measurement remains incomplete if no uncertainty interval is associated to the result.

The **measurand** is the quantity intended to be measured. According to ISO/IEC 17025, the measurement result depends on the whole **measurement complex**: instrument, operator, environmental conditions and the measurand itself — not on the instrument alone.

blocks.title: Compatible vs different results
blocks.text:
To transfer the information contained in two measures, their uncertainty intervals must be given.
- **Overlapping bands** $\Rightarrow$ **compatible** results: it is *not* possible to say which is higher; their difference can be just an effect of random errors.
- **Non-overlapping (separated) bands** $\Rightarrow$ **not compatible**: you can state the two results are different ($A>B$).
It is **never** possible to establish an equality condition; at best results are *compatible*. The guide for evaluating uncertainty is the **GUM** (JCGM 100:2008); the vocabulary is the **VIM** (JCGM 200:2012).
2. Measurement errors: systematic and random

**Measurement error** = measured quantity value minus a reference quantity value. It splits into two components:
- **Systematic error** (VIM 2.17): in replicate measurements it remains **constant or varies in a predictable manner**.
- **Random error** (VIM 2.19): in replicate measurements it **varies in an unpredictable manner**. Random errors are the residuals of systematic errors and other influencing factors; being many, random and of similar entity, they are managed with statistical methods based on the **normal distribution**.

blocks.title: Worked: a systematic error you can correct
blocks.text:
Temperature affects the dimensional measurement of a steel piece through thermal expansion: $L=L_0\,[1+\alpha\,(T-T_0)]$, so the error is $\Delta L=L_0\,\alpha\,(T-T_0)$.

With $L_0=100$ mm, $\alpha=11\times10^{-6}\ \mathrm{K^{-1}}$, $T-T_0=10\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$:
$$\Delta L=100\times11\times10^{-6}\times10=0.011\ \mathrm{mm}=11\ \mu\mathrm{m}.$$
Knowing $T$ and $\alpha$, this **predictable** error can be **corrected** by subtracting it — that is exactly why it is *systematic*, not random.

blocks.title: Uncertainty = what remains
blocks.text: After systematic errors are corrected/compensated/eliminated and measurement accidents are excluded, the **measurement uncertainty** is built from what is left — the residuals of systematic errors plus the random errors. Averaging reduces the random variability of the mean.
3. Trueness, precision, accuracy

**Measurement trueness** = closeness of agreement between the **average of an infinite number of replicate** measured values and a **reference** value. Trueness is **inversely related to systematic error** and **not** related to random error. Its numeric counterpart (with opposite sign) is the **bias** (VIM 2.18) = estimate of a systematic measurement error. Do NOT call trueness 'accuracy'.

**Measurement precision** = closeness of agreement between indications obtained by **replicate measurements** on the same/similar objects under specified conditions. It is expressed numerically by measures of **im**precision (standard deviation, variance, coefficient of variation). The 'specified conditions' may be **repeatability**, intermediate precision, or **reproducibility** conditions.

**Measurement accuracy** = closeness of agreement between a **measured** value and the **true** value of the measurand. It accounts for the **total variability** (it depends on *both* trueness and precision). Accuracy is **not** a quantity and is **not** given a number; a measurement is 'more accurate' when it has a smaller error. Do NOT use 'accuracy' to mean trueness, nor 'precision' to mean accuracy.

blocks.title: The dartboard picture
blocks.text: **Trueness** = the centre of the cluster is on the bull's-eye (small *systematic* error). **Precision** = the darts are tightly grouped (small *random* spread), regardless of where the centre is. **Accuracy** = darts both tight *and* centred — it requires good trueness AND good precision.
4. Repeatability vs reproducibility

Same **measurement procedure**, same **operators**, same **measuring system**, same operating conditions and same **location**, with replicate measurements on the same object **over a short period of time**. Repeatability is strongly linked to the instrument and represents an **ideal condition** (disturbances avoided as much as possible). Example: applying the *same group of masses* slowly to a force transducer, with maximum care, a single operator, in a short time.

**Different** locations, **different** operators, **different** measuring systems (possibly different procedures), with replicate measurements on the same object. Reproducibility represents better the **real working conditions**. Example: a force transducer evaluated through a standard force machine, repositioned several times, rotated, and at least once disassembled, packed, reassembled and put back.

question.prompt: A single technician measures the same shaft 20 times in 10 minutes with one micrometer, never changing anything. The dispersion of these 20 values estimates the instrument's:
question.explanation: Same operator, same instrument, same place, short time, replicates on the same object — these are exactly **repeatability** conditions, so the spread estimates repeatability. **A** reproducibility would require *changing* operators / instruments / locations. **C** trueness is the closeness of the *average* to a reference and needs a standard, not just spread. **D** resolution is the smallest perceptible change in the indication, a fixed instrument property, not a spread of repeated readings.
question.theory: Repeatability = everything kept the same, short time. Reproducibility = conditions deliberately changed. Both are expressed through the dispersion (precision) of the results.

blocks.text: 5. Metrological characteristics of instruments

**Smallest change in a quantity being measured that causes a perceptible change in the corresponding indication.** For an **analogue scale** the line thickness is about $1/2$, $1/5$ or $1/10$ of the elementary division, so the resolution is $1/2$, $1/5$ or $1/10$ of that division (read as $r=e/n$ with $e$ the division and $n$ the number of subdivisions). For a **digital scale** the resolution corresponds to the **maximum increment** — the largest difference between two subsequent indications reported in increasing order.

**Quotient of the change in an indication and the corresponding change in the value of the quantity being measured.** It matters for instruments with different input/output units; for a **direct-reading** instrument the sensitivity is always **1**. Example: a load cell where force (N) is the input and electric tension (µV) the output — a cell of $3\ \mu\mathrm{V/N}$ is *more sensitive* than one of $2\ \mu\mathrm{V/N}$. Do **not** confuse sensitivity with resolution.

**Stability** = property whereby the metrological characteristics of an instrument **remain constant in time**. The opposite is **drift**: e.g. the **zero instability** of a scale varies over time and affects *every* measurement (the zero shift propagates to the whole scale). It is checked by reading with no load or a stable reference weight, and is often overcome by **restoring the zero** with a reset operation.

- **Range** (measuring interval): the set of values of the measurand the instrument can measure within stated limits.
- **Linearity / linearity error**: how well the input–output relation follows a straight line; a deviation is a **systematic** *linearity error*.
- **Hysteresis error**: the indication for a given measurand value depends on whether that value was approached by **increasing** or **decreasing** the input — a **systematic** error linked to the instrument.

blocks.title: Which errors are systematic, which random?
blocks.text: From the lesson's table of specific errors: **reading (parallax) error** and **mobility error** → *random*; **inversion, linearity, hysteresis** errors → *systematic*; **zero error** → *random–systematic*. Parallax (a reading error of analogue scales) is avoided with a **blade pointer** or a **mirror** scale that signals the correct, orthogonal observation direction.
6. Calibration, traceability, metrological confirmation

An operation that, under specified conditions, first establishes a **relation between the quantity values with measurement uncertainties** provided by measurement standards and the corresponding **indications** (with their uncertainties), and then uses this relation to obtain a **measurement result from an indication**. Calibration is the basis of traceability.

Property of a measurement result whereby the result can be related to a reference through a **documented, unbroken chain of calibrations**, each contributing to the measurement uncertainty. The traceability chain climbs from the plant's process instruments → plant working/reference standards → accredited / national / international calibration laboratories → toward the true value. Quality standards (ISO/IEC 17025) require instruments to be **traceable** to national standards so that results are **compatible** with each other.

blocks.title: Certification vs accreditation; metrological confirmation
blocks.text: **Metrological confirmation** is the set of operations (calibration + verification + adjustment/repair + re-verification + sealing/labelling) needed to ensure a measuring instrument **conforms to the requirements for its intended use**. We speak of **certification** of the quality of a factory (ISO 9001) and **accreditation** of a calibration or test laboratory (ISO/IEC 17025). When the product *is* a measure, its characteristics are completely defined only by considering its **uncertainty** — which is why the measurement-specific rules differ from the general quality rules.

practice.prompt: Why is a measurement result considered **incomplete** if reported as a single number?

options.content: Because the instrument is always broken.
Because the value is distorted by systematic and confused by random factors, so it must be accompanied by an uncertainty interval.
Because only the average of infinite measurements is the true value.
Because numbers cannot represent physical quantities.

practice.explanation: Every result is distorted by systematic factors and confused by random ones, so it must be given as a **value plus its uncertainty interval** — only then can the information be correctly transferred and results compared. **A** is false; instruments work but are never perfect. **C** describes trueness, not the reason a single number is incomplete. **D** is wrong — numeric data do represent quantities, just imperfectly.
practice.theory: A measurement result = value + uncertainty interval. Without the interval the information is incomplete (GUM principle).

practice.prompt: Block A is measured as $30.0003\pm0.0004$ mm and block B as $29.9996\pm0.0004$ mm. What can you conclude?

options.content: A is definitely longer than B.
The uncertainty bands overlap, so the results are compatible — you cannot say which is longer.
The measurement is invalid because the values differ.

practice.explanation: A's band is $[29.9999,\,30.0007]$ and B's is $[29.9992,\,30.0000]$; they share the point $30.0000$, so the **bands overlap** and the results are **compatible** — their difference can be a random effect, and you cannot state which is longer. **A** would need *separated* bands. **B** is wrong: it is never possible to establish equality, only compatibility. **D** is wrong — differing values are normal, not invalidating.
practice.theory: Overlap of uncertainty bands ⇒ compatible (cannot rank). Separation ⇒ different ($A>B$). Equality can never be proven.
practice.source: Lesson 1 (transfer of information; overlap vs separation)

practice.prompt: A thermometer always reads $2\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ too high. This error is:

options.content: systematic, and can be corrected by subtracting the known offset
random, and reduced by averaging many readings
a measurement accident to be excluded

practice.explanation: A constant, predictable offset is a **systematic error**; knowing it, you **correct** the result by subtracting it. **B** is wrong: averaging removes random scatter, not a constant bias. **C** an accident is a rare, large, isolated value, not a constant offset. **D** resolution is the smallest perceptible change in the indication, unrelated to an offset.
practice.theory: Systematic error: constant or predictable in replicates; reduced by correction, compensation or elimination. Random error: unpredictable; reduced by averaging.

practice.prompt: An instrument is highly **precise** but its readings cluster well **away** from the reference value. The instrument has:

options.content: good precision but poor trueness (hence poor accuracy)

practice.explanation: Tight clustering = good **precision** (small random spread); offset from the reference = poor **trueness** (large systematic error / bias). Accuracy needs *both*, so accuracy is poor. **A** contradicts the offset. **B** contradicts the tight clustering. **D** resolution is a separate property, not implied by precision or bias.
practice.theory: Precision = closeness among replicates (random). Trueness = closeness of the average to a reference (systematic, inverse of bias). Accuracy = both together.

practice.prompt: Two people, using two different micrometers in two different rooms, each measure the same gauge block. The spread of their results estimates the instrument's:
practice.explanation: Different operators, different instruments, different locations on the same object = **reproducibility** conditions, which better represent real working conditions. **A** repeatability would keep operator, instrument, place and time fixed. **C** sensitivity is the output-change / input-change quotient. **D** trueness needs comparison of the average to a reference standard.
practice.theory: Reproducibility deliberately varies operators/instruments/locations; repeatability keeps everything the same over a short time.

practice.prompt: A digital indicator shows the successive readings 120, 122, 125 mm. From these, the instrument's **resolution** is taken as:
practice.explanation: For a digital scale the resolution corresponds to the **maximum increment** between subsequent indications: increments are $122-120=2$ and $125-122=3$, so the resolution is **3 mm**. **A** is invented. **B** takes the minimum, not the maximum. **D** is a reading, not an increment.
practice.theory: Digital resolution = maximum increment between consecutive indications in increasing order. Analogue resolution = $e/n$ (a fraction 1/2, 1/5, 1/10 of the division).

practice.prompt: A load cell converts force (N) into voltage (µV). What does its **sensitivity** of $3\ \mu\mathrm{V/N}$ describe, and how does it compare to a $2\ \mu\mathrm{V/N}$ cell?

options.content: The smallest force it can resolve; the 3 µV/N cell resolves finer.
Its zero drift; the 3 µV/N cell drifts more.
Its range; the 3 µV/N cell has a wider range.
The output change per unit input change; the 3 µV/N cell is more sensitive.

practice.explanation: Sensitivity = (change in indication)/(change in measurand), i.e. **output per unit input**; a larger value, $3\ \mu\mathrm{V/N}>2\ \mu\mathrm{V/N}$, means **more sensitive**. **A** confuses sensitivity with resolution. **B** and **C** invent unrelated meanings (drift, range). For a direct-reading instrument the sensitivity would be 1.
practice.theory: Sensitivity (VIM 4.12) is the input–output gain; it is 1 for direct-reading instruments and is distinct from resolution.

practice.prompt: A dial gauge gives 5.02 mm when the spindle is pushed **up to** a step and 5.05 mm when it is **let down to** the same step. This $0.03$ mm difference is a:
practice.explanation: An indication that depends on whether the measurand was approached **increasing** or **decreasing** is the signature of **hysteresis**, classified in the lesson as a **systematic** error. **A** parallax is a *random* reading error tied to viewing direction, not to approach direction. **B** resolution is the smallest perceptible change, not a direction-dependent gap. **C** zero drift is a slow change of the zero over *time*, not with the direction of loading.
practice.theory: Hysteresis: the reading depends on the direction of approach (loading vs unloading); it is a systematic instrument error, like the linearity and inversion errors.
practice.source: Lesson 3 (specific measurement errors; hysteresis is systematic)

practice.prompt: Metrological **traceability** of a measurement result requires:

options.content: a documented, unbroken chain of calibrations to a reference, each contributing to the uncertainty
that the instrument be brand new
that no uncertainty is associated with the result

practice.explanation: Traceability (VIM 2.41) is the property whereby a result can be related to a reference through a **documented, unbroken chain of calibrations**, each step adding to the uncertainty — the chain that climbs from plant instruments to national/international standards. **B** age is irrelevant. **C** is the opposite: every link *contributes* uncertainty. **D** describes a repeat measurement, not traceability.
practice.theory: Traceability = unbroken, documented calibration chain to a reference; it underpins the comparability (compatibility) of results between laboratories.

practice.prompt: On an analogue scale, the **parallax (reading) error** is reduced by:

options.content: using a blade pointer or a mirror scale to fix the orthogonal observation direction

practice.explanation: Parallax arises when the observation direction is **not orthogonal** to the scale plane; a **blade pointer** (which looks narrowest when viewed correctly) or a **mirror** scale (correct when pointer and reflected image overlap) fixes the right direction. **A** range is unrelated. **B** calibration handles systematic offsets/traceability, not the operator's viewing angle. **C** averaging digital readings does not concern an analogue parallax error.
practice.theory: Parallax is a random reading error of analogue scales tied to viewing direction; blade pointers and mirror scales reveal the correct, orthogonal line of sight.
